1: Ps 54: Davidic Lament — The Ziphite Betrayal. The superscription links to two occasions when the Ziphites (Judahites from Ziph in the Judean desert) informed Saul of David's whereabouts: 1 Sam 23:19 and 26:1. The betrayal by fellow-Judeans — not Philistines or Canaanites — was particularly bitter: the informers were from David's own tribe. 'Save me, O God, by your name' — bemishpecha (by your name) — the divine name as the active agency of rescue. The name YHWH is not merely a label but the embodiment of divine character and power; invoking the name is invoking the full weight of YHWH's covenant identity.
2: 'Hear my prayer, O God; give ear to the words of my mouth' — the double petition for divine auditory attention. Tefillati (my prayer) + imrei fi (words of my mouth) — both the structured prayer and the specific verbal content are presented. The sanctuary context: petitions were offered in the direction of the Jerusalem temple, where YHWH's presence was localized (1 Kgs 8:28-30).
3: 'For strangers have risen against me; ruthless men seek my life; they do not set God before them' — zarim (strangers/insolent ones — the term is ambiguous; some manuscripts read zedim, the arrogant/presumptuous). The Ziphite informers were technically Israelites but are called 'strangers' because their betrayal placed them outside the covenant community of loyalty. 'They do not set God before them' — the practical atheism formula (cf. Ps 53:1): not formal unbelief but behavior that ignores YHWH's moral order.
4: 'Behold, God is my helper; the Lord is the upholder of my life' — the pivot from petition to confidence: hinneh Elohim ozer li (behold, God is my helper). Ezer (helper — the Gen 2:18 word, used of YHWH as Israel's helper: Exod 18:4; Deut 33:29). 'The upholder of my life' (somech naphshi) — samach (to lean on, support) is the same word for laying hands on the sacrifice (Lev 1:4): YHWH bears the weight of the psalmist's life as the offerer bears on the sacrifice.
5: 'He will return the evil to my enemies; in your faithfulness put an end to them' — the lex talionis petition: yashuv hara le-shorerai (return the evil upon my foes). The divine faithfulness (emet, truth/reliability) is invoked as the mechanism of justice: the same reliable character that makes YHWH's promises trustworthy also makes his judgments trustworthy. The prayer for enemy destruction is grounded not in vindictiveness but in covenant justice.
6: 'With a freewill offering I will sacrifice to you; I will give thanks to your name, O LORD, for it is good' — nedavah (freewill offering, voluntary sacrifice — from nadav, to volunteer willingly). The freewill offering was not commanded by schedule but offered by voluntary impulse of gratitude, usually in response to rescue or blessing. The vow is proleptic: made before the rescue in confident anticipation. Ki tov (for it is good) — the goodness of the divine name (= divine character) is the ground of thanksgiving.
7: 'For he has delivered me from every trouble, and my eye has looked in triumph on my enemies' — the past-tense assurance of rescue: ki mikol-tzarah hitzilani (for from every trouble he has delivered me) — perfect tense expressing certainty. 'My eye has looked in triumph on my enemies' (uveoyvai reatah eini) — the idiom of the eye seeing what it desired: the vindication of the psalmist is visible. The psalm closes in the register of completed confidence despite the ongoing external threat, the characteristic move of lament psalmody.
